story.title: Liberty, Equality, Blood
story.subtitle: A Chronicle of the French Revolution, 1789–1799

theme.ambientPrompt: A candlelit engraving in the style of a late 18th-century French revolutionary print: tricolor cockades, crowded Parisian streets, the Bastille silhouetted against a stormy sky, rendered in sepia ink and muted red and gold washes on aged parchment.

scenes.title: The Old Order Cracks
scenes.narration: Long before the mobs and the guillotine, France groaned beneath a system centuries old: a kingdom of privilege, exemption, and unpaid debt. By the 1780s the treasury was empty, the harvests were failing, and the men who ruled from Versailles could no longer patch over the cracks. This is where the story begins — not with a single spark, but with a slow-building pressure that no minister could relieve.

citations.snippet: Economic recession from 1785, combined with bad harvests in 1787 and 1788, led to high unemployment and food prices, causing a financial and political crisis.

scenes.caption: A pre-revolutionary map of the French provinces, still divided by ancient jurisdictions
scenes.narration: This map shows the France that the Revolution would dismantle: a patchwork of provinces, each with its own customs, courts, and tax rates, some still governed as if they were separate kingdoms. Border regions like Béarn, Navarre, and Franche-Comté kept old privileges the crown had never fully absorbed. Reform meant untangling centuries of local law — one reason simplifying taxation proved so fiercely resisted by provincial parlements.

citations.snippet: numerous border provinces...were autonomous or belonged to the Holy Roman Empire, the Crown of Aragon or the Kingdom of Navarra

scenes.narration: The road to crisis ran through failed reforms. Calonne's tax plan was rejected by nobles handpicked to approve it; his successor Brienne fared no better against the parlements. Each refusal pushed the monarchy closer to summoning a body it had ignored for 175 years — the Estates-General, last seen in the reign of Louis XIII's father.

citations.snippet: Summoned by King Louis XVI, it opened on 5 May 1789 at Versailles, marking the first such meeting since 1614.

scenes.quoteText: We have need of a concourse of our faithful subjects, to assist us surmount all the difficulties we find relative to the state of our finances
scenes.attribution: Royal edict summoning the Estates-General, 24 January 1789
scenes.narration: In the king's own words, issued from Versailles, lies the confession of a government at the end of its rope. Louis XVI asked his 'faithful subjects' for help he could no longer manufacture himself. The edict summoned deputies from every bailliage and sénéchaussée in the realm — a vast, untested electoral machine set in motion by desperation rather than design.

citations.snippet: Nous avons besoin du concours de nos fidèles Sujets, pour nous aider à surmonter toutes les difficultés où nous nous trouvons relativement à l'état de nos finances

scenes.caption: Jérôme Pétion (1753–1794), a Third Estate deputy who would rise with the Revolution's radical tide
scenes.narration: Men like Jérôme Pétion arrived at Versailles as obscure provincial lawyers and left as architects of a new nation. Elected among the Third Estate's deputies, he found himself part of a chamber ordered to vote separately from the clergy and nobility — a structure guaranteeing the common people could always be outvoted despite representing the overwhelming majority of France.

citations.snippet: the clergy and nobility could combine to outvote the Third Estate, despite representing less than 5% of the population

scenes.quoteText: the deputies voted 491 to 89 to adopt the title National Assembly
scenes.narration: Six weeks of deadlock ended in an act of open defiance. Following a motion by the Abbé Sieyès, the Third Estate declared itself no longer bound to the old order of separate chambers, casting off the very name 'Estates' and proclaiming itself the National Assembly — the voice, they insisted, of the nation itself.

citations.snippet: the deputies voted 491 to 89 to adopt the title National Assembly

chapters.title: The Bastille and the August Fires

scenes.title: The Bastille and the August Fires
scenes.narration: In the sweltering July of 1789, rumor and fear did what pamphlets could not: they turned deputies into revolutionaries and Parisians into an army. A fortress fell, a governor's head rode a pike through the streets, and within weeks a thousand years of feudal privilege were burned away in a single overnight session of the Assembly. France would never again be governed as it had been governed before.

citations.snippet: On 14 July many of these soldiers joined a crowd attacking the Bastille, a royal fortress with large stores of arms and ammunition.

scenes.caption: Georges Danton (1759–1794), orator and revolutionary politician
scenes.narration: Georges Danton's booming voice would soon fill the halls of revolutionary Paris, but in the summer of 1789 he was one of thousands swept up in a city gone electric with rumor. Word spread that the king meant to use his Swiss Guards to crush the Assembly; crowds poured into the streets, and soldiers of the Gardes Françaises refused to fire on them. The stage was set for the Bastille.

citations.snippet: On 12 July, the Assembly went into a non-stop session following rumours that the king was planning to use the Swiss Guards to force it to close.

scenes.caption: The storming of the Bastille, 14 July 1789
scenes.narration: The fighting cost eighty-three attackers their lives before the governor, Bernard-René de Launay, surrendered. He was marched to the Hôtel de Ville and killed by the crowd, his head raised on a pike and paraded through Paris. Inside the fortress rumored to hold hundreds of prisoners, the mob found only seven — four forgers, a lunatic, a failed assassin, and one wayward nobleman. Symbol mattered more than substance; the Bastille fell, and with it the myth of royal invincibility.

citations.snippet: Although rumoured to hold many prisoners, the Bastille held only seven: four forgers, a lunatic, a failed assassin, and a deviant nobleman.

events.label: Lynching of Foullon and his son
The Great Fear sweeps rural France

scenes.narration: Violence did not stay confined to Paris. Rumors of aristocratic plots ignited a countryside panic historians call the Great Fear — peasants forming militias, burning manor records, attacking chateaux they believed sheltered brigands hired by nobles. Faced with a nation unraveling, the Assembly chose a radical remedy: abolish the very system peasants were rebelling against.

citations.snippet: The season of La Grande Peur – the Great Fear – was characterised by social hysteria and anxiety over who was going to be the next victim.

scenes.quoteText: The National Assembly abolishes the feudal system entirely.
scenes.attribution: National Constituent Assembly, night session, 4 August 1789
scenes.narration: Through a single overnight session, nobles and bishops rose one after another to renounce their own privileges — hunting rights, tithes, seigneurial dues, venal offices, provincial exemptions — until, as one deputy proposed, the Assembly declared the whole feudal edifice dissolved. A medal was struck to commemorate the night; Louis XVI himself was named 'Restorer of French Liberty,' though he privately seethed at the loss.

citations.snippet: The National Constituent Assembly, after deliberating on the night of 4 August 1789, announced, "The National Assembly abolishes the feudal system entirely."

scenes.quoteText: I will never consent to the despoliation of my clergy and my nobility ... I will never give my sanction to the decrees that despoil them
scenes.attribution: Louis XVI, private letter following the August Decrees
scenes.narration: Publicly gracious, privately furious — Louis XVI's own letter reveals the gulf between what the Revolution demanded and what the king could bear to accept. He praised the 'noble and generous demarche' of clergy and nobility even as he vowed never to truly sanction their despoiling. That contradiction, more than any mob, would prove fatal to his throne.

citations.snippet: I will never consent to the despoliation of my clergy and my nobility ... I will never give my sanction to the decrees that despoil them

scenes.title: From Crown to Republic
scenes.narration: By 1792 the compromise of constitutional monarchy had collapsed under the weight of war, suspicion, and a king who could not stop conspiring against the very revolution he was meant to lead. The storming of the Tuileries, the fall of the crown, and finally the fall of a king's own head — this chapter follows France's violent leap from monarchy into republic, and from republic into terror.

citations.snippet: On 22 September the Convention replaced the monarchy with the French First Republic and introduced a new calendar, with 1792 becoming "Year One".

scenes.caption: The storming of the Tuileries Palace, 10 August 1792
scenes.narration: Military defeat abroad and royal betrayal at home converged on a single August morning. Crowds and National Guardsmen stormed the Tuileries Palace, overwhelming the king's Swiss Guard in brutal street fighting. Within weeks the monarchy that had ruled France for centuries would be formally abolished — the insurrection of 10 August proved the point of no return.

citations.snippet: military defeats after the outbreak of the French Revolutionary Wars in April 1792 led to the insurrection of 10 August 1792

scenes.caption: The Battle of Valmy, 20 September 1792
scenes.narration: Just ten days after the Tuileries fell, ragtag French volunteers stood their ground against seasoned Prussian regulars at Valmy — and won. It was the first great victory of the Revolutionary Wars, and it emboldened the National Convention. Two days later, on 22 September, they abolished the monarchy outright and declared the First Republic, resetting the calendar itself to 'Year One.'

citations.snippet: On 20 September, the French defeated the Prussians at the Battle of Valmy, in what was the first major victory by the army of France during the Revolutionary Wars.

scenes.caption: The execution of Louis XVI, Place de la Révolution, 21 January 1793
scenes.narration: Citoyen Louis Capet, once King Louis XVI, was condemned by a Convention divided almost evenly on his guilt. Letters found in his own hand, conspiring with royalist exiles, sealed his fate: 361 deputies voted for death, 288 against. On 21 January 1793, on the square now called the Place de la Concorde, the blade fell — and the Revolution crossed a threshold from which there was no retreat.

citations.snippet: On 17 January 1793, Louis was sentenced to death for "conspiracy against public liberty and general safety". 361 deputies were in favour, 288 against

events.label: September Massacres begin in Paris jails
First Republic proclaimed, Year One begins
France declares war on Britain and the Dutch

scenes.narration: The winter of 1792 into 1793 compressed a revolution's worth of upheaval into a few violent months: mob executions in the jails of Paris, the fall of a thousand-year monarchy, the death of a king, and a widening war that would soon draw in Britain, Spain, and half of Europe. In the conservative Vendée, rural France rose against Paris itself, horrified by the changes forced upon its churches.

citations.snippet: already unsettled by changes imposed on the church, in March the traditionally conservative and royalist Vendée rose in revolt

scenes.caption: Olympe de Gouges, author of the Declaration of the Rights of Woman and of the Female Citizen
scenes.narration: Not every revolutionary voice was male. Olympe de Gouges insisted that the Revolution's promises of liberty and equality must extend to women too — in matters of divorce, property, and the recognition of illegitimate children. Yet the National Assembly denied women political rights outright, and de Gouges herself would be executed in November 1793, a Girondist swept up in the Terror she had helped inspire debate about.

citations.snippet: Along with other Girondists, she was executed in November 1793 during the Terror.

scenes.title: The Terror
scenes.narration: In the spring of 1793 the Republic, besieged by foreign armies and rebellion at home, handed nearly dictatorial power to a twelve-man committee sworn to save it by any means. What followed was the bloodiest chapter of the Revolution: a Tribunal that sentenced sixteen thousand people to death, a guillotine that became popular entertainment, and a Jacobin leadership that ultimately devoured its own architects.

citations.snippet: About 16,000 people were sentenced by the Revolutionary Tribunal and executed in the Reign of Terror, which ended in July 1794 with the Thermidorian Reaction.

scenes.caption: The Death of Marat, by Jacques-Louis David, 1793
scenes.narration: Jean-Paul Marat, the fiery journalist whose newspaper L'Ami du peuple had stoked revolutionary fervor for years, was stabbed to death in his medicinal bath by Charlotte Corday, a Girondin sympathizer who blamed him for the Revolution's spiraling violence. David's painting transformed his murdered body into something close to a martyr's icon — proof that even amid factional slaughter, the Revolution still knew how to make saints of its dead.

citations.snippet: radical Montagnards, headed by Robespierre, Georges Danton, and Jean-Paul Marat

scenes.quoteText: chronic violence, ambivalent forms of justice, and repeated recourse to heavy-handed repression
scenes.attribution: Description of the revolutionary government's methods
scenes.narration: The instrument of this repression was the Committee of Public Safety, formed on 6 April 1793 and granted sweeping executive power by December. Charged with defeating foreign armies and the Vendée revolt alike, it ruled through emergency decree, its members answerable to no fixed law but the survival of the Republic itself.

citations.snippet: the directory was characterised by "chronic violence, ambivalent forms of justice, and repeated recourse to heavy-handed repression."

scenes.caption: Jean-Marie Collot d'Herbois (1750–1796), member of the Committee of Public Safety
scenes.narration: Men like Collot d'Herbois sat on the twelve-man Committee that, by law, held supervisory power over the armed forces, the judiciary, and the ministers of the Convention itself. Robespierre was only one voice among these administrators of the Terror, though history would remember his name above all others as the Revolutionary Tribunal's sentences climbed into the thousands.

citations.snippet: the committee was given broad supervisory and administrative powers over the armed forces, judiciary and legislature, as well as the executive bodies and ministers of the convention

events.label: ~16,000 executed by Revolutionary Tribunal since Terror began

scenes.narration: The machinery Robespierre helped build eventually turned on him. After the arrest and execution of rival Hébertist and Dantonist factions, suspicion in the Convention curdled against Robespierre himself. On 27 July 1794 — 9 Thermidor by the revolutionary calendar — he was arrested; the next day, the guillotine that had claimed sixteen thousand others claimed him too.

citations.snippet: After the arrest and execution of rival factions of Hébertists and Dantonists, sentiment in the Convention eventually turned against Robespierre, who was executed in July 1794.

scenes.caption: The execution of Robespierre and Saint-Just, 28 July 1794
scenes.narration: The crowd that gathered to watch Robespierre die was no smaller than those that had watched his victims. Vendors still sold programmes naming the condemned; the tricoteuses still knitted at the scaffold's edge. But this execution marked an ending: the Thermidorian Reaction that followed dismantled the machinery of Terror and, within months, replaced the Committee's rule with a new and fragile government — the Directory.

citations.snippet: knitting women (tricoteuses) formed a cadre of hardcore regulars, inciting the crowd. Parents often brought their children.

chapters.title: The Directory and the Rise of Bonaparte

scenes.title: The Directory and the Rise of Bonaparte
scenes.narration: Weakened by war, inflation, and its own contradictions, the Committee of Public Safety gave way in November 1795 to a five-man Directory — a government that pleased almost no one and satisfied fewer. Royalists plotted its overthrow, Jacobins despised its corruption, and radicals like Babeuf dreamed of a more equal republic. Into this instability stepped a young general whose troops had already proven decisive on more than one Parisian street.

citations.snippet: Jacobin sympathisers viewed the French Directory as a betrayal of the Revolution, while Bonapartists later justified Napoleon's coup by emphasizing its corruption.

scenes.caption: Paul Barras, a leading director of the French Directory
scenes.narration: Paul Barras stood at the center of the Directory's shifting alliances — Republican enough to resist royalist restoration, pragmatic enough to survive coup after coup. It was Barras who first recognized the usefulness of a young artillery officer named Napoleon Bonaparte, whose cannon fire against a royalist rising in Paris on 5 October 1795 proved the Convention could still command loyal, decisive force.

citations.snippet: On 5 October, Convention troops led by Napoleon put down a royalist rising in Paris

scenes.quoteText: over 100 of the 150 new deputies were royalists of some sort
scenes.narration: Even after crushing the royalist rising, the Directory could not crush royalist sentiment itself. When the first legislative elections under the new constitution were held just two weeks later, the majority of new deputies leaned toward restoring the monarchy — proof that the Republic's enemies had not vanished, only retreated into the ballot box.

citations.snippet: when the first legislative elections were held two weeks later, over 100 of the 150 new deputies were royalists of some sort

events.label: Directory replaces Committee of Public Safety
Conspiracy of the Equals crushed, Babeuf executed
Coup of 18 Fructidor annuls royalist election gains
Coup of 18 Brumaire ends the Directory

scenes.narration: The Directory's four turbulent years swung wildly between left and right. Babeuf's Conspiracy of the Equals demanded a more radical, equitable republic and was crushed within weeks. Royalist victories at the polls two years later were annulled by force, with sixty-three leading royalists deported to French Guiana. Fighting continued despite exhaustion on every side, until Napoleon's coup of 18 Brumaire finally swept the whole fragile structure away.

citations.snippet: The elections were annulled, 63 leading Royalists deported to French Guiana, and laws were passed against émigrés, Royalists and ultra-Jacobins.

scenes.caption: Cambacérès (1753–1824), jurist who would help shape the post-revolutionary legal order
scenes.narration: As the Directory faltered, men like Cambacérès worked to salvage what could survive of the Revolution's legal achievements — the end of feudal privilege, the principle of equality before the law. When Napoleon's Consulate replaced the Directory in 1799, it was builders like these who ensured the Revolution's core reforms, if not its republican spirit, endured into the new century.

citations.snippet: Napoleon as emperor set up a constitutional system and the restored Bourbons were forced to retain one.

scenes.quoteText: Revolution became a tradition, and republicanism an enduring option.
scenes.attribution: Historian Paul Hanson, on the legacy of the Revolution
scenes.narration: The Bastille's stones were long since scattered, the Terror's scaffolds dismantled, and the Directory itself swept aside by 1799 — yet something irreversible had happened to France. Feudal privilege did not return under Napoleon, nor under the restored Bourbons, nor under any government after. Equality before the law, once declared in a single overnight session in August 1789, had become the permanent floor beneath French society, whatever government stood upon it.

citations.snippet: Revolution became a tradition, and republicanism an enduring option.
